steps.title: Recall what makes two lines parallel
steps.content: When a transversal $k$ crosses lines $r$ and $s$, the lines are parallel if and only if one of these conditions holds: corresponding angles are equal, alternate interior angles are equal, or co-interior (same-side interior) angles sum to $180°$. We know $w = 160°$ at the intersection of $k$ and $r$. We need the single additional fact that proves $r \parallel s$.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — $x = 20$: $x$ is the supplement of $w$ at the intersection with $r$ (they form a straight angle: $160 + 20 = 180°$). This is information already determined by $w = 160$ alone — it tells us nothing new about $s$. ✗
  • Choice B — $y = 160$: $y$ is the angle at the intersection of $k$ and $s$ in the same position as $w$ at $r$ — they are corresponding angles. If $w = y = 160°$, corresponding angles are equal, which is the definition of parallel lines. ✓
  • Choice C — $w + y = 180$: This would mean $y = 20°$. Co-interior angles summing to $180°$ would require $w$ and the interior angle on the same side at $s$ to sum to $180°$ — but $w + y = 180$ makes $y$ a supplement of $w$, placing $y$ in the alternate-exterior relationship, not the co-interior one. This condition does not correspond to any standard parallel-line theorem. ✗
  • Choice D — $y + z = 180$: $y$ and $z$ are supplementary angles at the intersection of $k$ and $s$ — they already sum to $180°$ by the straight-angle postulate, regardless of whether $r$ and $s$ are parallel. This is always true and gives no information. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
